content.text: Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS) is a Windows Server role that acts as a Security Token Service (STS) and Claims Provider. When a domain is federated with Entra ID, Entra redirects sign-in requests for that domain's users to the AD FS farm. AD FS authenticates the user against Active Directory and issues a signed SAML assertion or WS-Fed token that Entra ID trusts and exchanges for its own JWT tokens.

Federation places the entire authentication path on-premises. Entra ID does not validate the user's credentials — it trusts whatever claims AD FS asserts. This makes AD FS the authoritative identity provider for federated domains, with Entra ID acting as a relying party.

attrs.alt: AD FS federation sign-in flow: 11 steps from browser to Entra ID JWT token via AD FS SAML/WS-Fed assertion

content.text: Why Organizations Use AD FS Federation

AD FS federation was the original hybrid identity method before PHS and PTA existed. Organizations still use it when they have requirements that PHS and PTA cannot satisfy: custom authentication logic implemented as AD FS claim rules, on-premises MFA adapters (RSA SecurID, Duo via AD FS adapter), smart card / certificate authentication that AD FS handles natively, or compliance requirements that prohibit any credential derivative from leaving the corporate network.

Microsoft's recommendation since 2019 is to migrate from AD FS to PTA or PHS. AD FS introduces significant operational overhead, certificate management complexity, and a larger attack surface. Organizations should evaluate whether remaining AD FS requirements can be met by Entra ID's native capabilities before investing in AD FS maintenance.

A managed domain (UPN suffix) can be converted to federated by pointing Entra ID at the AD FS farm's metadata URL. Once federated, all sign-in requests for that UPN suffix are redirected to AD FS. Entra Connect can configure this automatically during setup, or you can do it manually via PowerShell.

# Check current domain authentication type
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Domain.Read.All'
Get-MgDomain -DomainId 'contoso.com' |
    Select-Object Id, AuthenticationType, IsVerified, IsDefault
# AuthenticationType: 'Managed' (PHS/PTA) or 'Federated' (AD FS)

# Convert a managed domain to federated (requires MSOnline module)
Connect-MsolService

# Get AD FS endpoints from the farm
$adfsServer = 'adfs.contoso.com'
$metadataUrl = "https://$adfsServer/federationmetadata/2007-06/federationmetadata.xml"

# Convert domain to federated
Convert-MsolDomainToFederated -DomainName 'contoso.com' `
    -SupportMultipleDomain  # required if you have multiple domains

# Or set federation manually with specific endpoints
Set-MsolDomainAuthentication `
    -DomainName 'contoso.com' `
    -Authentication Federated `
    -PassiveLogOnUri "https://$adfsServer/adfs/ls/" `
    -ActiveLogOnUri "https://$adfsServer/adfs/services/trust/2005/usernamemixed" `
    -MetadataExchangeUri "https://$adfsServer/adfs/services/trust/mex" `
    -SigningCertificate '<base64-encoded-token-signing-cert>' `
    -IssuerUri "http://contoso.com/adfs/services/trust" `
    -LogOffUri "https://$adfsServer/adfs/ls/?wa=wsignout1.0"

# Verify federation settings
Get-MsolDomainFederationSettings -DomainName 'contoso.com' | Format-List

AD FS issues tokens containing claims — name-value pairs about the authenticated user. You define claim rules that extract attributes from Active Directory, transform them, and include them in the token. Entra ID has its own claims pipeline, but custom claims from AD FS can flow through the federation trust and influence downstream application behavior.

# View and manage AD FS relying party trust for Entra ID (Office 365)
Import-Module ADFS

# List all relying party trusts
Get-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust | Select-Object Name, Identifier, IssuanceTransformRules |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Get claim rules for the Microsoft 365 relying party
$rpt = Get-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -Name 'Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform'
$rpt.IssuanceTransformRules

# Example: Add a custom claim rule to include department in the token
$existingRules = $rpt.IssuanceTransformRules
$newRule = @'
c:[Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/windowsaccountname",
   Issuer == "AD AUTHORITY"]
=> issue(store = "Active Directory",
   types = ("http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/claims/Department"),
   query = ";department;{0}",
   param = c.Value);
'@

Set-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -TargetName 'Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform' `
    -IssuanceTransformRules ($existingRules + $newRule)

# View claim descriptions (all claim types AD FS knows about)
Get-AdfsClaimDescription | Where-Object ShortName -match 'department' |
    Select-Object Name, ClaimType, ShortName
Migrating from AD FS to Managed Authentication

Microsoft provides a staged migration path from AD FS to PHS or PTA. The migration uses Entra staged rollout to move users one group at a time from federated to managed authentication, allowing you to validate the experience before converting the entire domain. This avoids the risk of a hard cutover.

# Staged migration from AD FS to PHS

# Step 1: Enable PHS (if not already enabled via Entra Connect)
Import-Module ADSync
Set-ADSyncAADPasswordSyncConfiguration -SourceConnector 'contoso.com' `
    -TargetConnector 'Entra ID Connector' -Enable $true
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta

# Step 2: Create a pilot group
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Group.ReadWrite.All','Policy.ReadWrite.FeatureRollout'
$pilotGroup = New-MgGroup -DisplayName 'PHS-Migration-Pilot' `
    -MailEnabled $false -SecurityEnabled $true -MailNickname 'phs-pilot'

# Add a test user to the pilot group
$testUserId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'testuser@contoso.com'").Id
New-MgGroupMember -GroupId $pilotGroup.Id -DirectoryObjectId $testUserId

# Step 3: Create staged rollout policy for PHS
$policy = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies' `
    -Body (@{
        displayName = 'AD FS to PHS Migration'
        feature     = 'passwordHashSync'
        isEnabled   = $true
        isAppliedToOrganization = $false
    } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Step 4: Add pilot group to staged rollout
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies/$($policy.id)/appliesTo/`$ref" `
    -Body (@{ '@odata.id' = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryObjects/$($pilotGroup.Id)" } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Step 5: Validate pilot users can sign in without AD FS
# (Pilot users authenticate via PHS; other users still go through AD FS)

# Step 6: Full domain cutover after validation
# Run on Entra Connect server:
# Set-MsolDomainAuthentication -DomainName 'contoso.com' -Authentication Managed
# All users now authenticate via PHS; AD FS is no longer involved

# Step 7: Decommission AD FS (after 30+ day observation period)
Write-Output 'Remove relying party trusts, uninstall AD FS roles, decommission WAP servers'
PHS enabled, all users still federated
Staged rollout: pilot group on PHS
Pilot users sign in via PHS
All users sign in via PHS/PTA
AD FS High Availability and Certificate Management

An AD FS farm typically consists of two or more AD FS servers behind a load balancer, plus Web Application Proxy (WAP) servers in the DMZ for external access. The most common operational failure is expired token signing or token decryption certificates. AD FS supports automatic certificate rollover for internally-managed certificates, but externally-provisioned SSL certificates must be renewed manually.

# Monitor AD FS certificate health
Import-Module ADFS

# Token signing certificates
Get-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType Token-Signing |
    Select-Object Thumbprint, IsPrimary,
        @{N='ExpiresIn'; E={($_.Certificate.NotAfter - (Get-Date)).Days}} |
    Format-Table

# Token decrypting certificates
Get-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType Token-Decrypting |
    Select-Object Thumbprint, IsPrimary,
        @{N='ExpiresIn'; E={($_.Certificate.NotAfter - (Get-Date)).Days}}

# SSL certificate on the federation service
(Get-AdfsSslCertificate).CertificateHash
(Get-AdfsSslCertificate | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Certificate).NotAfter

# Manually roll over token signing certificate
# AD FS auto-rollover handles this if AutoCertificateRollover = true
(Get-AdfsProperties).AutoCertificateRollover

# Update Entra ID with the new token signing certificate after rollover
# (Entra Connect does this automatically; manual update via MSOnline):
Connect-MsolService
Update-MsolFederatedDomain -DomainName 'contoso.com' -SupportMultipleDomain

# Check Entra ID has the current certificate thumbprint
(Get-MsolDomainFederationSettings -DomainName 'contoso.com').SigningCertificate
Web Application Proxy (WAP) for Extranet Access

Web Application Proxy is the supported reverse proxy for AD FS, running in the perimeter network. It proxies /adfs/* requests from the internet to the internal AD FS farm without exposing AD FS directly. WAP also supports pre-authentication for published internal applications.

# Install Web Application Proxy role on perimeter server
Install-WindowsFeature Web-Application-Proxy -IncludeManagementTools

# Configure WAP to point at AD FS farm
Install-WebApplicationProxy `
  -FederationServiceTrustCredential (Get-Credential) `
  -CertificateThumbprint '<SSL_THUMBPRINT>' `
  -FederationServiceName 'adfs.contoso.com'

# Publish the AD FS /adfs/* endpoints through WAP
Add-WebApplicationProxyApplication `
  -Name 'AD FS Proxy' `
  -ExternalPreAuthentication PassThrough `
  -ExternalUrl 'https://adfs.contoso.com/' `
  -BackendServerUrl 'https://adfs.contoso.com/'

# Verify WAP configuration
Get-WebApplicationProxyApplication | Select-Object Name, ExternalUrl, BackendServerUrl

# Publish an internal app with AD FS pre-authentication
Add-WebApplicationProxyApplication `
  -Name 'Intranet Portal' `
  -ExternalPreAuthentication ADFSforRichClients `
  -ADFSRelyingPartyName 'IntranetPortal' `
  -ExternalUrl 'https://portal.contoso.com/' `
  -BackendServerUrl 'http://intranet.corp.contoso.com/'

# Check WAP connectivity health
Get-WebApplicationProxyHealth

WAP is a Windows Server role, not a standalone product. Each WAP server trusts the AD FS service via a trust certificate established at install time. Replace WAP with Azure AD Application Proxy when migrating to managed auth.
Monitoring AD FS with Entra Connect Health

Entra Connect Health provides a cloud-based monitoring dashboard for AD FS farms. An agent installed on each AD FS server (and each WAP server) streams audit events and performance data to Microsoft. The portal shows failed sign-ins, risky IP reports, and relying party usage analytics without requiring on-premises log aggregation.
Install the Connect Health for AD FS agent on every AD FS server and WAP node.
Enable verbose AD FS audit logging and the Application Generated audit policy so the agent has events to ship.
Use the Risky IP Report to identify credential-spray attacks targeting the extranet endpoint.
The Usage Analytics blade breaks down token issuance by application, user, and error code.
Alert thresholds can be configured per service — e.g., alert when token-issuance failures exceed 5% in a 15-minute window.

# Install Entra Connect Health for AD FS agent on each AD FS server
# Download MSI from portal.azure.com > Microsoft Entra ID > Connect Health > Quick start

# Register the AD FS agent (run on each AD FS server)
Register-AzureADConnectHealthADFSAgent

# Verify agent status after registration
Get-AzureADConnectHealthAgentStatus

# List monitored AD FS services in your tenant
Get-AzureADConnectHealthADFSService

# Retrieve recent risky sign-in report from Connect Health
# (also visible in portal.azure.com > Connect Health > AD FS > Risky IP report)
Get-AzureADConnectHealthADFSRiskyIPReport -StartDate (Get-Date).AddDays(-7) -EndDate (Get-Date)

# Pull usage analytics per application
Get-AzureADConnectHealthADFSUsageMetrics -RelyingPartyId 'urn:federation:MicrosoftOnline'

# AD FS audit logging — enable on each AD FS server for Connect Health to ingest
Set-AdfsProperties -LogLevel Verbose
auditpol /set /subcategory:'Application Generated' /success:enable /failure:enable

# Review failed token-issuance events in Windows Event Log (Event ID 501, 325)
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'AD FS/Admin' | Where-Object { $_.Id -in 501,325 } | Select-Object -First 20
Extranet Smart Lockout and Brute-Force Protection

AD FS 2019 and later includes Extranet Smart Lockout (ESL), which tracks familiar IP locations per user. Sign-ins from known IPs are treated more leniently than sign-ins from unknown locations. This prevents a distributed password-spray attack (which rotates IPs) from locking out legitimate users who use a consistent office IP.
Deploy ESL in Audit (log-only) mode for at least one week before switching to Enforce mode.
Set ExtranetLockoutThreshold to a value above your legitimate failed-attempt rate (15 is common for enterprise).
Reset-AdfsAccountLockout clears ESL state only — you must also unlock the AD account separately if it became locked.
Monitor Event IDs 1201 (soft lockout from unfamiliar IP) and 1203 (hard lockout) in the AD FS/Admin log.

# Enable AD FS Extranet Smart Lockout (replaces legacy extranet lockout)
# ESL distinguishes familiar vs unknown locations before locking the account

# Check current lockout settings
Get-AdfsProperties | Select-Object ExtranetLockoutEnabled, ExtranetLockoutThreshold,
    ExtranetObservationWindow, ExtranetLockoutRequirePDC

# Enable ESL in Audit mode first (no blocking, only logging)
Set-AdfsProperties -EnableExtranetLockout $true `
                   -ExtranetLockoutMode ADFSSmartLockoutLogOnly `
                   -ExtranetLockoutThreshold 15 `
                   -ExtranetObservationWindow (New-TimeSpan -Minutes 30)

# After reviewing logs, switch to Enforce mode
Set-AdfsProperties -ExtranetLockoutMode ADFSSmartLockoutEnforce

# Review ESL audit events (Event ID 1203, 1201)
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'AD FS/Admin' | Where-Object { $_.Id -in 1201,1203 } | Select-Object -First 20

# Unlock a specific user from ESL (does NOT unlock AD account)
Reset-AdfsAccountLockout -Identity 'CORP\jsmith'

# Classic extranet lockout (legacy, for older AD FS 2016 without ESL)
Set-AdfsProperties -EnableExtranetLockout $true `
                   -ExtranetLockoutThreshold 5 `
                   -ExtranetObservationWindow (New-TimeSpan -Minutes 30)
Token Lifetime and SSO Session Management

AD FS issues several types of tokens: SAML 2.0 assertions for federated applications, OAuth 2.0 access/refresh tokens for modern clients, and SSO cookies for browser sessions. Each has an independently configurable lifetime. Getting lifetimes wrong leads to either excessive re-authentication prompts or security gaps from long-lived tokens.

# View current AD FS global token lifetime settings
Get-AdfsProperties | Select-Object TokenLifetime, SsoLifetime, DeviceSsoEnabled,
    PersistentSsoLifetimeMins, PersistentSsoEnabled, SsoAuthenticationRequestParameters

# Adjust SSO session cookie lifetime (default 480 minutes)
Set-AdfsProperties -SsoLifetime 480

# Adjust access token lifetime for a specific relying party
Set-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -TargetName 'Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform' `
    -TokenLifetime 60   # minutes; 0 = use global default

# Enable persistent SSO (keep-me-signed-in) — requires device registration
Set-AdfsProperties -PersistentSsoEnabled $true `
                   -PersistentSsoLifetimeMins 10080  # 7 days

# View all relying party trusts with custom token lifetimes
Get-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust | Select-Object Name, TokenLifetime | Where-Object { $_.TokenLifetime -ne 0 }

# Set Client Access Policy — block legacy authentication for Office 365
Set-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -TargetName 'Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform' `
    -IssueOAuthTokensTo AllBrowserBasedTokenRequestsOnly

# SAML artifact resolution lifetime (for IdP-initiated flows)
Set-AdfsProperties -ArtifactDbConnection 'Data Source=.\MICROSOFT##WID;...'
Set-AdfsSamlProtocolResolution -LifeTime 300   # seconds

AD FS federation with Entra ID gives organizations full control over the authentication experience — custom claim rules, on-premises MFA adapters, and smart card support. The tradeoff is substantial operational complexity: farm infrastructure, WAP servers, certificate management, and an on-premises dependency at every sign-in. Microsoft recommends migrating to managed authentication (PHS or PTA) when possible, using staged rollout for a safe, reversible transition.
Federated domains redirect all sign-ins to AD FS; Entra ID trusts and exchanges the signed assertion for its own tokens
AD FS is the right choice for custom claim rules, on-premises MFA adapters, and smart card authentication
The main operational risks are certificate expiry and farm availability — both cause immediate sign-in failures for all federated users
Entra Connect must update Entra ID when token signing certificates roll over, or authentication fails
Staged rollout lets you migrate groups of users to PHS without a hard domain cutover
After converting a domain to managed, wait 30+ days before decommissioning AD FS in case rollback is needed
Conditional Access policies apply in Entra ID after AD FS issues its assertion — MFA can be enforced by Entra even for federated domains
